691	\begin{proof}
692	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
693	  Take \(X\) a scheme, \(\mathcal{B}\) the basis of affine opens, the admissible
694	  covers the standard affine covers, and \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Fix an
695	  affine open \(B = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \in \mathcal{B}\) and a standard cover
696	  \(\mathcal{U} : B = \bigcup_i D(f_i)\); we show \(H^k(B, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all
697	  \(k \geq 1\) and that the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison is an isomorphism,
698	  via the acyclic-resolution route.
699	
700	  Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{U}\) on \(B\),
701	  \[
702	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\; \cdots,
703	    \qquad
704	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} (j_\sigma)_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_\sigma}\bigr),
705	  \]
706	  the product running over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections \(U_\sigma =
707	  D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\). By
708	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} this augmented complex is exact, so
709	  \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\); its exactness is the
710	  prime-local contracting homotopy of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, with no
711	  sheaf-cohomology input.
712	
713	  Each term \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is acyclic for the global-sections functor
714	  \(G = \Gamma(B, -)\). Indeed each \(U_\sigma = D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\) is a basic
715	  open of \(B\), hence affine, and the higher cohomology of
716	  \((j_\sigma)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_\sigma})\) on \(B\) is computed by the module-level
717	  {\v C}ech complex of a standard cover of the affine \(U_\sigma\), which is exact in
718	  positive degrees by the same contracting homotopy of
719	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} applied one step down to \(U_\sigma\); a product
720	  of \(G\)-acyclic objects is \(G\)-acyclic. There is no spectral sequence and no
721	  appeal to sheaf cohomology — only standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing.
722	
723	  Apply the acyclic-resolution comparison
724	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with \(G = \Gamma(B, -)\) and the
725	  resolution \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\): for every \(k\),
726	  \[
727	    H^k(B, \mathcal{F}) \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(\Gamma(B, \mathcal{C}^\bullet)\bigr)
728	      \;=\; \check{H}^k(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}).
729	  \]
730	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the right-hand {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes
731	  for \(k \geq 1\), whence \(H^k(B, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\); the comparison
732	  map \(\check{H}^k(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to H^k(B, \mathcal{F})\) is then an
733	  isomorphism (both sides vanish for \(k \geq 1\), and in degree \(0\) both equal
734	  \(\Gamma(B, \mathcal{F})\)). The argument is non-circular: it takes narrowed
735	  standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}) as input and
736	  lifts it to affine sheaf vanishing as output, with no use of
737	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}.
738	\end{proof}

740	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
741	
742	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
743	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
744	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
745	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
746	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
747	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.

792	  Exactness of a complex of sheaves is a local question on \(X\), so it may be
793	  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
794	  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =

805	\begin{lemma}
806	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
807	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
808	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isSheafify_presheafCohomology}
809	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
810	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01XJ, Lemma
811	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
812	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
813	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
814	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
815	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
816	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
817	  % associated to the presheaves
818	  % $$
819	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
820	  % $$
821	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
822	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
823	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
825	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
826	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
827	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For every \(k \geq 0\) the higher direct image
828	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{G})\) is the sheaf
829	  on \(S\) associated to the presheaf
830	  \[
831	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
832	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
833	  \]
834	  with the evident restriction maps. In particular \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) vanishes
835	  iff \(H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for every \(V\) in a basis of
836	  the topology of \(S\); since the affine opens form such a basis, it suffices to
837	  check the vanishing on affine \(V\). This is the local-on-the-base description
838	  through which \(f_*\)-acyclicity is reduced to absolute cohomology vanishing on
839	  preimages.
840	\end{lemma}
841	
842	\begin{proof}
843	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
844	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
845	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
846	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\); since the abelian
847	  structure on \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules computes cokernels and kernels objectwise on
848	  the level of presheaves and then sheafifies, that cohomology sheaf is the
849	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
850	  = H^k\bigl(\mathcal{I}^\bullet(f^{-1}(V))\bigr) = H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\),
851	  the last equality because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is an injective (hence
852	  acyclic) resolution computing the absolute cohomology of \(f^{-1}(V)\). Evaluation
853	  of a sheafification vanishes exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis,
854	  giving the stated affine-local criterion.
855	\end{proof}

869	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
870	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.}
871	  Let \(X\) be separated, \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open immersion of an affine
872	  open \(U\), and \(f : X \to S\) any morphism, with composite \(g := f \circ j : U
873	  \to S\). Then:
874	  \begin{enumerate}
875	    \item \(j\) is an affine morphism (an open immersion of an affine open into a
876	      separated scheme), so its higher direct images vanish: \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} = 0\)
877	      for all \(q \geq 1\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
878	      \(\mathcal{H}\); and
879	    \item consequently, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}\) on \(U\) and every
880	      \(k \geq 0\),
881	      \[
882	        R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \;\cong\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H}.
883	      \]
884	  \end{enumerate}
885	\end{lemma}

900	  (1) An open immersion of an affine open \(U\) into a separated scheme \(X\) is an
901	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
902	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
903	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
904	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
905	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
906	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
907	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
908	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
909	
910	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
911	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
912	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
913	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
914	
915	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
916	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
917	  sheafification of
918	  \[

920	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
921	  \]
922	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
923	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
924	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
925	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
926	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
927	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing

929	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
930	  Since the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), this forces

943	  the middle equality because \(f_* \circ j_* = (f \circ j)_* = g_*\), and the last
944	  because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(\mathcal{H}\)
945	  computing \(R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\).
946	\end{proof}
947	
948	\begin{lemma}
949	[Each {\v C}ech term is $f_*$-acyclic]
950	  \label{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}
951	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic}
952	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
953	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
954	  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct
955	  % images for affine morphisms) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
956	  % L180--199).
957	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
958	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
959	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
960	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
961	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative affine vanishing).}
962	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
963	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\mathfrak{U}\) a finite affine open
964	  cover of \(X\) with all intersections affine. Then for every \(p \geq 0\) the
965	  degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech term \(\mathcal{C}^p =
966	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\)
967	  is right \((\,\cdot\,)_*\)-acyclic for the pushforward \(f_*\):
968	  \[
969	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0
970	      \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
971	  \]
972	\end{lemma}
973	
974	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
975	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
976	% the sheaf
977	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
978	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
979	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
980	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
981	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
982	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
983	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
984	% opens we win."
985	\begin{proof}
986	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
987	  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
988	  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
989	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
990	  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
991	  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
992	  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
993	  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
994	  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
995	  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
996	  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
997	  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).
998	
999	  Now decompose the structure map \(g_s := f \circ j_s : U_s \to S\). First,
1000	  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
1001	  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
1002	  \(q \geq 1\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} part (2),
1003	  \(R^k f_*\bigl((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\bigr) \cong
1004	  R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\). Second, working locally over an affine
1005	  \(V \subseteq S\) as above, \(g_s\) restricts to a morphism of affine schemes
1006	  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
1007	  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
1008	  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing
1009	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): \(R^k(g_s)_*\mathcal{G}\) is the
1010	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
1011	  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
1012	  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
1013	  \(k \geq 1\).
1014	\end{proof}
1015	
1016	\begin{lemma}
1017	
1018	\leanok
1019	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
1020	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
1021	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
1022	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
1023	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
1024	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
1025	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
1026	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
1027	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
1028	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
1029	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
1030	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
1031	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
1032	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
1033	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
1034	  % $$
1035	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
1036	  % $$
1037	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
1038	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
1039	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
1040	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
1041	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
1042	  % we have
1043	  % $$
1044	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
1045	  % $$
1046	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
1047	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
1048	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
1049	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
1050	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
1051	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
1052	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
1053	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
1054	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
1055	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1056	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
1057	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
1058	  \[
1059	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
1060	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
1061	  \]
1062	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
1063	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
1064	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
1065	
1066	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
1067	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
1068	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
1069	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
1070	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
1071	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
1072	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
1073	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
1074	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
1075	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
1076	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
1077	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
1078	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
1079	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
1080	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
1081	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
1082	  role in the applications.
1083	\end{lemma}
1084	
1085	\begin{proof}
1086	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
1087	  Apply the abstract Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with the
1088	  right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
1089	  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
1090	  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
1091	  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. The two hypotheses of the
1092	  abstract lemma are exactly the geometric sub-lemmas established above:
1093	  \begin{enumerate}
1094	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
1095	      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
1096	      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
1097	      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
1098	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
1099	      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
1100	      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
1101	  \end{enumerate}
1102	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
1103	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
1104	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
1105	  resolution,
1106	  \[
1107	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
1108	      \;\cong\;
1109	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
1110	  \]
1111	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
1112	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
1113	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
1114	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
1115	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
1116	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
1117	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
1118	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
1119	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
1120	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
1121	  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
1122	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
1123	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).
1124	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}

61	
62	# Strategy Critic
63	
64	You are the fresh-context strategy critic. You read the project's `STRATEGY.md` with **no exposure to its iter-by-iter history** and challenge the strategic choices as if a mathematician encountering this project for the first time would.
65	
66	Your job is to be the project's adversarial reader. The plan agent has been in the project's context for many iterations and is naturally invested in the existing routes. You are the corrective.
67	
68	## Your context discipline
69	
70	Your directive will name ONLY:
71	
72	- The current `STRATEGY.md` (the primary subject of your review).
73	- The project's stated final goal (one paragraph naming the theorem(s) to formalize).
74	- A short reference index (`references/summary.md` content).
75	- A blueprint summary (chapter titles + one-line topic per chapter).
76	
77	Your directive will NOT include `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, prover task results, review summaries, or recent narrative. **If the directive accidentally includes any of these, ignore them.** Your value depends entirely on the lack of pollution. Reading the iter-by-iter history would make you the planner; the planner already exists. Stay fresh.
78	
79	You may use:
80	
81	- The references named in the directive (read them if needed for the math).
82	- The blueprint chapter summaries (the prose summary in the directive, NOT the full chapter text — full-chapter audit is the territory of the blueprint-review subagent in the catalog).
83	- `archon-lean-lsp` if you need to spot-check a Mathlib name's existence.
84	
85	You may NOT use:
86	
87	- Iter sidecars, even if you find their paths.
88	- Any state file other than what the directive named.
89	
90	## What you check
91	
92	For each strategic route in `STRATEGY.md`:
93	
94	1. **Goal-alignment.** Does this route, if executed, actually produce the project's stated final goal? Or is there a subtle gap where the route's end-state isn't quite what the goal demands?
95	
96	2. **Mathematical soundness.** Is the route's mathematical argument plausible? Are there steps that look like they assume something the route's prerequisites don't give you? A fresh reader is good at spotting "wait, this step needs X but X isn't established."
97	
98	3. **Alternative routes.** Are there other approaches the strategy doesn't mention? For each, briefly note: what it would look like, why it might be cheaper or sounder, and why the strategy may have rejected it (if you can tell from prose).
99	
100	4. **Sunk-cost reasoning.** Does the strategy justify a route in terms of "we've already done X" rather than "X is the right way"? That's a sunk-cost smell. Call it out by name.
101	
102	5. **Prerequisite assumptions.** Does the strategy assume Mathlib infrastructure that may not exist? Verify the named lemmas / type classes / structures actually exist (use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for spot-checks). Strategy that depends on phantom Mathlib infra is invalid.
103	
104	6. **Effort estimates.** If the strategy carries per-route LOC or iteration estimates, do they look honest given the scope of the route? Estimates that are wildly under-counted (e.g. "200 LOC for representability of Pic") indicate either underestimated effort or a misplanned route. The LOC cell is a rough remaining-LOC range (e.g. `~80–220`); flag rows whose LOC range is grossly inconsistent with `Iters left` given the route's scope — e.g. a sprawling representability phase marked `~30` LOC with `Iters left: 1`. Cross-check the realized LOC of comparable rows in `## Completed` (when present) against the active estimates: a remaining phase of similar scope to a completed one but estimated at a fraction of its realized LOC is a dishonest-estimate signal.
105	
106	7. **Infrastructure-deferral patterns.** This is a distinct failure mode from sunk-cost and must be checked independently. An infrastructure-deferral pattern is present when any of the following hold:
107	
108	   - A route pivot changes the surface approach but the hardest prerequisite (a missing Mathlib construction, an unproven foundational lemma, a typeclass that doesn't exist) is the same before and after the pivot. The pivot renames the problem without solving it. To check: identify the hardest prerequisite in the current route and the hardest prerequisite in the route it replaced. If they are the same construction or the same gap, the pivot is avoidance.
109	
110	   - A required construction is described as "off-critical path", "future work", "deferred pending upstream Mathlib PR", "exceptional case", or similar language, while the project's stated goal provably requires it. The project's goal is not negotiable; if a construction is necessary for the final theorem, it is on the critical path by definition, regardless of what the strategy claims. Check by asking: does the final theorem statement in `## Goal` hold without the deferred construction? If no, the deferral is a goal weakening dressed as a strategic decision.
111	
112	   - The strategy splits the goal into a "main case" and one or more "exceptional cases", proves or plans to prove the main case, and defers the exceptional cases indefinitely. Unless the exceptional cases are genuinely independent theorems not required by `## Goal`, this is a goal weakening. Check the statement in `## Goal` — if it asserts something for ALL cases, each case is required.
113	
114	   - The strategy describes building infrastructure for a weaker or different statement than `## Goal` requires, with the intention of extending later. Plans requiring later extension of core infrastructure almost never complete the extension — flag as CHALLENGE.
115	
116	   - The strategy proposes huge phases and is reluctant to start working on them, without decomposing them into concrete sub-phases that it could have started on. This is a deferral pattern because it will always assume this phase it too difficult, but in pratice it should think like a mathlib contributor and decompose the phase into intermediate phases.
117	
118	   - A phase row in `## Phases & estimations` has `Iters left: ?` (or an estimate-free / never-shrinking cell) AND has been in that state without any active prover lane and without any concrete progress in the blueprints. Stagnant phases are infrastructure-deferral by inaction.
119	
120	   For each infrastructure-deferral finding: name the specific construction being deferred, confirm whether the stated goal requires it, and state whether any route in the strategy actually builds it with a concrete timeline. A construction deferred to "Mathlib upstream" with no project-side plan and no timeline is an unresolved gap in the project's strategy, not an accepted dependency.
121	
122	8. **Parallelism under-exploitation.** If the project has multiple independent files or proof obligations that are not sequentially dependent, but the strategy routes them through a single sequential phase, flag this as a throughput risk. Parallelism in prover dispatch is a planning obligation, not an optimization. A strategy that serializes independent work is implicitly estimating 2–3× the needed iter count.
123	
124	9. **Format compliance.** `STRATEGY.md` must follow the canonical skeleton documented in the plan prompt. Violations to flag:
125	
126	   - **Size**: the file exceeds ~250 lines or ~12 KB.
127	   - **Headings**: the section list isn't exactly `## Goal`, `## Phases & estimations`, `## Completed`, `## Routes`, `## Open strategic questions`, `## Mathlib gaps & new material` (in that order). `## Completed` is optional — omitted while nothing is done yet — but when present it MUST sit between `## Phases & estimations` and `## Routes`. Renamed or other extra top-level sections (`## Project goal`, `## End-state`, `## Decomposition`, `## Roadmap`, `## Soundness rules`, etc.) are violations.
128	   - **Per-iter narrative**: references to specific iterations ("this iter we tried X", "last iter", "the iter-XYZ pivot") in prose. Per-iter history belongs in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, never in STRATEGY.md. (Bare iter numbers in the `## Completed` table's `Iters` cell — e.g. `294 · 8` — are fine; that is the ledger, not narrative.)
129	   - **Accumulation discipline**: a completed phase still sitting in the active `## Phases & estimations` table (it must MOVE to `## Completed`), an excised route still occupying a `## Routes` subsection, or freeform prose-history anywhere. The concise `## Completed` table is allowed and expected; what's flagged is completed work left in the active table, prose bloat, or a `## Completed` table that has ballooned past ~12 rows or grown multi-line cells.
130	   - **Table discipline**: `## Phases & estimations` must be a Markdown table with columns Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks, one short line per cell. `Status` must be a short inline tag (`ACTIVE`, `BLOCKED`, `PAUSED BY USER`, …), not prose; `LOC` must be a rough remaining-LOC range (e.g. `~80–220`), not a velocity figure. When present, `## Completed` must be a Markdown table with columns Phase | Iters (done@ · used) | LOC | Files | Key results | Reusable techniques | Pitfalls, again one short line per cell. Long prose in cells, or replacing either table with prose subsections, is a violation.
131	   - **Appendix sections**: "Historical decisions", "Considered alternatives", "Past iterations summary", "Lessons learned", or any other history-tracking section. Iter sidecars are where rejected alternatives live.
132	
133	   Format violations are reported under a synthetic "format" route — see the Report Format section below. **Format is not cosmetic.** A STRATEGY.md that drifts from the canonical skeleton bleeds into the plan agent's context every iter and makes the strategy itself harder to reason about. Treat material format violations as a CHALLENGE that must be resolved this iter via an in-place restructure (using iter sidecars to hold any historical detail that currently lives inline).
134	
135	## Directive Format
136	
137	```markdown
138	# Strategy Critic Directive
139	
140	## Slug
141	<slug>
142	
143	## Project goal
144	<one paragraph: the final theorem(s) or claim(s) the project is trying to formalize. Include the protected declarations if any.>
145	
146	## Strategy under review
147	
148	<paste the entire STRATEGY.md verbatim.>
149	
150	## References index
151	
152	<paste references/summary.md verbatim.>
153	
154	## Blueprint summary
155	
156	<for each blueprint chapter, one line: filename + topic. NOT the chapter content — just the index.>
157	
158	## Prior critique status
159	
160	<one line per still-live prior-iter challenge. Format STRICTLY:
161	
162	  - <prior-iter-NNN>: <short challenge phrase> — live | addressed
163	
164	NO paraphrased planner responses, NO multi-paragraph re-litigation,
165	NO "the iter-XXX plan-agent considered pulling this to iter-YYY but
166	deferred to iter-ZZZ" — those are iter-by-iter narrative and would
167	contaminate the fresh-context audit. The point of "Prior critique
168	status" is solely to tell you which challenges to re-check, NOT how
169	the planner reasoned about them. If a prior critique is fully resolved
170	in the current STRATEGY.md, mark it "addressed"; if you disagree
171	after auditing, your report will flip it back to live.
172	
173	If first-iter (no prior critique), write "no prior critique".>
174	```
175	
176	**If you find this section contains planner-side reasoning, iter
177	narrative, or paraphrased plan-agent responses beyond the one-line
178	"live | addressed" tags, ignore that extra material.** Your fresh
179	context is your value; do not let leaked planner state colonize
180	your audit.
181	
182	## Report format
183	
184	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/strategy-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
185	
186	**Omit-empty rule.** Every section below is optional except `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict`. If a section's right answer is "nothing to report", **OMIT the section entirely** — do NOT write "none", "N/A", "no findings detected", or "(omit if empty)" as filler content. The absence of a section IS the signal that nothing was found there. Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is SOUND with no flagged items, render the block as just the verdict line and omit the bullet checklist above it.
187	
188	```markdown
189	# Strategy Critic Report
190	
191	## Slug
192	<slug>
193	
194	## Iteration
195	<NNN>
196	
197	## Routes audited
198	
199	For each strategic route in STRATEGY.md, one block:
200	
201	### Route: <name>
202	
203	- **Goal-alignment**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
204	- **Mathematical soundness**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
205	- **Sunk-cost reasoning detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name the sunk-cost claim verbatim>
206	- **Infrastructure-deferral detected**: yes | no — <if yes: name the deferred construction; state whether the goal requires it; state whether any route in the strategy builds it with a concrete timeline>
207	- **Phantom prerequisites**: <list any Mathlib infra the strategy assumes exists that you couldn't verify>
208	- **Effort honesty**: <reasonable | under-counted | over-counted> — <one line>
209	- **Parallelism under-exploited**: yes | no — <if yes, name the independent obligations being serialized>
210	- **Verdict**: SOUND | CHALLENGE | REJECT
211	  - SOUND: the route makes sense and the planner should proceed.
212	  - CHALLENGE: the route has issues the planner must address (in STRATEGY.md or via an explicit rebuttal in plan.md) before this iter ends.
213	  - REJECT: the route is fundamentally broken (goal-misaligned, mathematically unsound, or built on phantom prerequisites). Do not proceed on this route until the strategy is rewritten.
214	
215	## Format compliance
216	
217	A separate block from "Routes audited" — this audits the *document* against the canonical skeleton, regardless of whether the strategic content is sound.
218	
219	- **Size**: <line count> / <bytes> — within budget | over budget (~250 lines / ~12 KB).
220	- **Headings**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, list the violating headings>.
221	- **Per-iter narrative detected**: yes | no — <if yes, quote one or two representative phrases verbatim>.
222	- **Accumulation detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name completed phases still in the active `## Phases & estimations` table (should be in `## Completed`), excised routes still present, prose-history bloat, or a `## Completed` table that has outgrown its bound>.
223	- **Table discipline**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, describe the deviation>.
224	- **Appendix sections**: <list any detected; omit field if none>.
225	- **Format verdict**: COMPLIANT | DRIFTED | NON-COMPLIANT
226	
227	## Infrastructure-deferral findings <!-- omit entire section if no deferral patterns detected -->
228	
229	Each infrastructure-deferral finding gets one block. Default severity CHALLENGE; escalate to REJECT when the deferral makes the stated goal unprovable without the deferred item.
230	
231	### Deferred: <construction name>
232	
233	- **Required by goal**: yes | no | partially — <one line>
234	- **Current plan for building it**: <what STRATEGY.md says, or "none — deferred with no project-side plan">
235	- **Timeline**: <concrete (iter estimate) | vague ("future work") | absent>
236	- **Verdict**: CHALLENGE | REJECT — <one line>
237	
238	## Alternative routes (suggested) <!-- omit entire section if no fresh alternatives -->
239	
240	### Alternative: <name>
241	
242	- **What it looks like**: <one paragraph>
243	- **Why it might be cheaper or sounder**: <one paragraph>
244	- **What the current strategy may have rejected**: <if guessable; otherwise "unclear">
245	- **Severity of the omission**: critical | major | minor
246	
247	## Sunk-cost flags <!-- omit entire section if none -->
248	
249	- `<verbatim quote>` — Why this is sunk-cost: <one sentence>. Recommendation: <reframe on merits>.
250	
251	## Prerequisite verification <!-- omit entire section if no Mathlib infrastructure to verify -->
252	
253	- `<Mathlib name>`: VERIFIED | MISSING | RENAMED (as X)
254	
255	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if zero CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts AND format COMPLIANT AND no infrastructure-deferral findings -->
256	
257	Every CHALLENGE, every REJECT, every NON-COMPLIANT format verdict, and every infrastructure-deferral CHALLENGE/REJECT lands here automatically. No under-classification.
258	
259	- Route <name>: CHALLENGE — <what the planner must address>.
260	- Route <name>: infrastructure-deferral CHALLENGE — <construction> required by goal, no concrete timeline. Planner must either build it this iter or produce a concrete plan with an iter estimate.
261	- Alternative <name>: critical omission — <one line>.
262	- Phantom prerequisite <name>: <one line>.
263	- Format: NON-COMPLIANT — <two or three most impactful deviations>. Restructure STRATEGY.md in-place this iter.
264	
265	## Overall verdict
266	
267	One paragraph. If infrastructure-deferral findings exist, name them explicitly — "the strategy defers X, which is required for the stated goal" must appear verbatim so the plan agent cannot overlook it.
268	```
269	
270	## Return value
271	
272	Your final assistant message:
273	
274	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts, <K> infrastructure-deferral findings, format=<COMPLIANT|DRIFTED|NON-COMPLIANT>`
275	- The path to your full report.
276	
277	## Reminders
278	
279	- **You are the project's adversarial reader.** Don't be polite. If a route is avoidance dressed as strategy, say so.
280	- **Infrastructure deferral is not strategy.** A construction required by the goal is on the critical path regardless of what the strategy labels it.
281	- **A pivot that moves the same hard problem one layer deeper is not a pivot.** Check the hardest prerequisite in the new route against the hardest prerequisite in the old route. If they match, flag it.
282	- **"Off-critical path" is a red flag, not a planning decision**, when the goal requires the deferred item.
283	- **Don't request more context.** Iter history is what you're meant to be free of.
284	- **Cite Mathlib precisely.** Use the LSP tools to verify before claiming a prerequisite exists or is missing.
285	- **Strict severity.** CHALLENGE and REJECT are must-fix; do not under-classify to keep momentum.
286	- **Be specific about alternatives.** "Maybe consider another approach" is useless. Name it.

2	name: mathlib-analogist
3	description: Read-only Mathlib advisor with two modes. (1) **API alignment** — given a project declaration or proposed design, locates Mathlib's idiom for the same situation, compares the project's path to it, and reports whether the project should align or deviate (with cost analysis). Catches parallel APIs, narrow definitions, and "we couldn't build the prerequisite so we made up a stand-in" patterns before they harden. (2) **Cross-domain inspiration** — when a structural problem (a categorical shape, a fixed-point argument, a sheaf condition, a descent argument, ...) is stuck, searches *distant* Mathlib areas (functional analysis ↔ algebraic geometry ↔ probability ↔ combinatorics) for the same shape and extracts the technique used there.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**,analogies/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - I have two modes. Choose by setting `## Mode: api-alignment` or
10	    `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration` in the directive header.
11	    Dispatch the right mode for the question:
12	
13	    - **api-alignment**: "should this declaration be a typeclass / a

15	      and we're building a parallel?". This is the default and matches

17	    - **cross-domain-inspiration**: "this categorical pattern has been
18	      failing for K iters, has Mathlib solved the SAME structural
19	      problem in a different mathematical area?". Use the cross-domain

21	
22	  - In api-alignment mode, I tell you whether Mathlib already does the
23	    same thing the right way and what the cost of NOT aligning would
24	    be. In cross-domain-inspiration mode, I bring back structural
25	    analogues (with citations) and the technique each used, so you

28	  ### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)

32	
33	  - You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the

35	    write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the

113	
114	# Mathlib Analogist
115	
116	You are the read-only Mathlib-analogist subagent with **two modes**: API alignment (the default) and cross-domain inspiration. The directive's `## Mode` line selects which.
117	
118	## Mode: api-alignment
119	
120	The plan agent points you at a project declaration, a proposed definition, or a design question, and asks "what would Mathlib do, and is the project doing that?". Your job:
121	
122	1. Identify the actual design decisions at play (often multiple compressed into one question).
123	2. Find how Mathlib has resolved analogous decisions.
124	3. Compare the project's path to Mathlib's idiom.
125	4. Report whether the project should align with the idiom — and, if not, what the cost of the divergence is.
126	
127	You do **not** rubber-stamp the project's choice. If Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project skipped it (most often because the prerequisite infrastructure looked hard), say so plainly. "It works" is not enough — the cost of API fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and code that can't compose with Mathlib is real.
128	
129	## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration
130	
131	The plan agent points you at a *structural problem* — a categorical, topological, algebraic, or analytic shape that's been resisting the project's current approach — and asks "has Mathlib solved this same shape somewhere else, possibly in a completely different mathematical field?". Your job:
132	
133	1. Distill the structural problem to its categorical / algebraic / topological core, stripping away the project's specific objects.
134	2. Search *broadly* across Mathlib for that shape. Cross domains aggressively — a sheaf condition on schemes shares structure with a sheaf condition on topological spaces; a descent argument in alg geom shares structure with a descent argument in functional analysis; a fixed-point setup in measure theory shares structure with order-theoretic Knaster–Tarski.
135	3. For each analogue you find, extract the *technique* used there (the tactic combination, the categorical lemma, the unbundling trick, the inductive step).
136	4. Map the technique back to the project's setting. State concretely what porting the technique would look like.
137	
138	You are explicitly NOT checking API alignment in this mode. The goal is to surface unfamiliar territory that may be borrowable, not to validate the project's existing definitions. Distant analogues are MORE valuable than nearby ones — the planner already knows the nearby ones.
139	
140	## Scope
141	
142	**One design question per invocation.** A design question may be broad ("how should we represent the Picard scheme?") or narrow ("instance-based vs. predicate-based"). Multiple Mathlib precedents in the SAME question are welcome. What you should NOT do is sprawl across unrelated decisions in one call.
143	
144	**Read-only.** You may read:
145	
146	- Project files (`.lean`, blueprint chapters, `references/`).
147	- Mathlib (via `archon-lean-lsp` MCP: `lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`, hover, signature lookup).
148	- Any existing `analogies/<slug>.md` summaries from prior calls.
149	
150	You may write:
151	
152	- `analogies/<slug>.md` — persistent design-rationale file future iters re-read.
153	- `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path) — your report.
154	
155	You may **NOT** modify project source, blueprint, or any state file.
156	
157	## Directive Format
158	
159	The directive begins with `## Mode: api-alignment` or `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration`. The remaining fields differ by mode.
160	
161	### Mode: api-alignment
162	
163	```markdown
164	# Mathlib Analogist Directive
165	
166	## Mode
167	api-alignment
168	
169	## Slug
170	<slug>
171	
172	## Design question
173	<the question. One question per directive.>
174	
175	## Project artifact(s) under question
176	- <file>:<lines> — <declaration or section>
177	- <file>:<lines> — <declaration or section>
178	
179	## Why now
180	<one or two sentences: what the dispatching agent is about to do (write, refactor, decide), and why a Mathlib precedent would inform it.>
181	
182	## Hints (optional)
183	<Mathlib namespaces, related concepts, or specific declarations the dispatcher suspects are relevant.>
184	
185	## Severity expectation
186	<one of: routine | high-stakes>
187	- routine: cheap sanity check on a small choice
188	- high-stakes: this design will be load-bearing; be strict about idiom adherence
189	```
190	
191	### Mode: cross-domain-inspiration
192	
193	```markdown
194	# Mathlib Analogist Directive
195	
196	## Mode
197	cross-domain-inspiration
198	
199	## Slug
200	<slug>
201	
202	## Structural problem
203	<the categorical / algebraic / topological shape, stated as abstractly
204	as possible. Strip away the project's specific objects — the goal is
205	to make the shape recognizable across domains. Example: "we need a
206	sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of modules over
207	a site whose covering family is finite flat" rather than "we need a
208	sheaf condition for quasi-coherent modules on Spec(O_K)".>
209	
210	## Failed approaches
211	<one short bullet per attempt that didn't work. Format:
212	- <approach>: <underlying obstacle that stopped it>.
213	- ...
214	
215	The goal is to tell me what NOT to suggest porting back — if the
216	analogue's technique boils down to "do exactly what failed", skip it.>
217	
218	## Search radius
219	<one of: wide | narrow>
220	- wide: search any Mathlib domain — functional analysis, probability,
221	  combinatorics, order theory, anything. Preferred for genuinely
222	  novel structural shapes.
223	- narrow: stay within the project's general area (e.g. algebraic
224	  geometry / commutative algebra / category theory) but cross
225	  sub-areas. Preferred when the planner suspects the analogue is
226	  "one shelf over" rather than across the library.
227	
228	## Hints (optional)
229	<Mathlib namespaces, technique names, or specific declarations the
230	dispatcher suspects share structure. Use sparingly — your value is
231	in finding analogues the dispatcher couldn't.>
232	```
233	
234	## Workflow
235	
236	1. **Read the directive completely.** Note the `## Mode` line first — your workflow branches on it.
237	
238	### api-alignment mode
239	
240	2. **Read the project artifact(s)** and their surrounding context — imports, neighboring declarations, the blueprint chapter the declaration corresponds to (per the `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug mapping). The blueprint often makes the mathematical intent explicit where the Lean only hints at it.
241	
242	3. **Identify the open design decision(s).** A directive that says "should this be a typeclass?" usually compresses several decisions (bundled vs unbundled, predicate vs structure, instance vs explicit). Name each.
243	
244	4. **Locate Mathlib precedents.** Use `lean_leansearch` and `lean_loogle` on the relevant names and types. Read 2–5 Mathlib files that resolve analogous decisions. For each, note:
245	   - What Mathlib chose (typeclass / structure / predicate / function).
246	   - Why (legible from the docstring, naming convention, or how the choice composes with surrounding API).
247	   - How the project's current or proposed approach compares.
248	
249	5. **Compare and judge.** For each open decision, write:
250	   - The Mathlib idiom (with citation: `Mathlib.X.Y.Foo`, line N).
251	   - The project's path (or proposed path).
252	   - The gap: identical, divergent-but-equivalent, divergent-with-cost, divergent-and-wrong.
253	   - For "divergent-with-cost" and "divergent-and-wrong": the concrete cost (bridge lemmas needed, downstream files that can't consume the project's API, parallel infrastructure that will eventually need to be unified).
254	
255	6. **Render a verdict per decision.** One of:
256	   - **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — the project should use the idiom; if shipped code diverges, refactor.
257	   - **DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY** — divergence is the right call for this project; document why in `analogies/<slug>.md`.
258	   - **PROCEED** — no Mathlib precedent applies; project's path is reasonable.
259	   - **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** — Mathlib doesn't have the idiom yet; the project must build it (this is the case for genuinely new infrastructure).
260	
261	7. **Write the persistent analogy file** to `analogies/<slug>.md`. Format below.
262	
263	8. **Write the report** to `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md`.
264	
265	### cross-domain-inspiration mode
266	
267	2. **Re-state the structural problem in your own words** as the most abstract version you can defend. Strip the project's objects. Aim for a one-paragraph re-statement that names only the categorical / topological / algebraic primitives.
268	
269	3. **Brainstorm analogues across Mathlib.** Before searching, list 5–10 mathematical situations *in other Mathlib domains* that share the same structural shape. Be aggressive about distance — if the problem is sheaf-shaped, look at probability sheaves AND topological sheaves AND order-theoretic sheaves. If it's a fixed-point setup, look at Knaster–Tarski (order theory), Banach (functional analysis), Schauder (topology), Kleene (lattice theory), and so on.
270	
271	4. **Verify analogues with the LSP.** For each candidate, use `lean_leansearch` and `lean_loogle` to confirm the construction actually lives in Mathlib. Read the Mathlib file. Discard candidates that turn out to be only superficially similar.
272	
273	5. **Extract the technique.** For each verified analogue, write:
274	   - The Mathlib citation (`Mathlib.X.Y.Foo`, line N).
275	   - The structural problem solved there, in the same abstract vocabulary you used in step 2.
276	   - The technique: the tactic combination, the key lemma, the categorical reformulation, the unbundling step — whatever the actual proof or construction depends on.
277	   - The mapping back: how would this technique translate to the project's setting? Be concrete — name the project's objects, point to the corresponding Mathlib constructs.
278	   - The cost of porting: typeclass scaffolding needed, lemmas to be ported, anything the Mathlib version assumes that the project doesn't have.
279	
280	6. **Skip analogues whose technique matches a "Failed approaches" bullet.** If the analogue's technique reduces to something the directive already tried, drop it.
281	
282	7. **Render a verdict per analogue.** One of:
283	   - **ANALOGUE_FOUND** — a Mathlib precedent with a portable technique. The planner should consider trying it next iter.
284	   - **PARTIAL_ANALOGUE** — Mathlib solves a related but not identical shape; the technique is suggestive but not directly portable. Worth a deeper look only if the higher-priority options fail.
285	   - **NO_USEFUL_ANALOGUE** — Mathlib has the shape but the technique doesn't generalize beyond Mathlib's specific setting.
286	
287	8. **Rank.** Order ANALOGUE_FOUND verdicts by porting cost (lowest first). The planner reads top-to-bottom.
288

## Directive Format

```markdown
# Blueprint Reviewer Directive

## Slug
<slug>

## Strategy snapshot
<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>

## Routes
<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>

## References
- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
- <arxiv ID>: <topic>

## Focus areas (optional)
<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>

## Known issues
<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
```

## What you do

1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
   - Read the entire chapter.
   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:
   - **wire-up** — it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding — recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.
   - **remove** — it is genuinely orphaned scaffolding: nothing in the goal's proof closure needs it, it isn't the goal, and it isn't a `\mathlibok`/reference anchor. Only then is removal the right call. Name the chapter + label so the plan agent can authorize a writer to delete it.
   - **keep** — it is intentionally standalone (the goal theorem with nothing above it, a deliberately isolated `\mathlibok` reference, etc.). Say why and move on.
   You **flag and recommend**; you never edit. The plan agent turns a `wire-up`/`remove` disposition into a blueprint-writer directive.
7. **Run the rendering lint.** `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once for the whole blueprint; triage every `malformed_refs` finding (`undefined-macro`, `math-delim`, `literal-ref`, `bare-label`) per the "Rendering integrity" bullet above, grouped per chapter.
8. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
9. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
10. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.

You may also use:
- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
- **`archon blueprint-doctor [--json]`** — the deterministic rendering/structure lint (orphan chapters, broken refs, literal-REF, interleaved math delimiters, bare labels, undefined macros, covers problems). Read-only; run it once per review and triage its `malformed_refs` (see "Rendering integrity" above).
- **`leandag`** — your read-only window into the real dependency DAG. Run it freely: it never touches project source or the blueprint. (`leandag build` refreshes the derived `.leandag/` cache, which is regenerated deterministically from the current tree — that is a tool artifact, not a project edit, so it does not violate your read-only rule.)
  - `leandag build --json` — the structured build report: `unknown_uses` (broken `\uses{}`), `unmatched_lean` (`\lean{}` pointing nowhere), `conflicts`, and `summary.isolated`.
  - `leandag stats` — counts including `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint`.
  - `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated [--type theorem] [--chapter <c>]`) — the isolated declarations, which you triage per node (below).
  - `leandag show gaps` — blueprint nodes missing a `\lean{}`.

You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.

## Report format

# Strategy Critic Directive

## Slug
pivot

## Project goal

Prove `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (protected, frozen signature): for `f : X ⟶ S` separated and quasi-compact, `F` quasi-coherent, and `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X`, the existence-form isomorphism `Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F)` (under `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`), where `higherDirectImage f i F = ((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F`. End-state: zero inline `sorry` in the cone, zero project axioms, kernel-only axioms. The downstream Picard/Quot machinery is out of scope (carved away in the extraction).

## Strategy under review

# Strategy

## Goal

Prove `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (`lem:cech_computes_cohomology`),
the protected, frozen-signature target: for `f : X ⟶ S` separated and quasi-compact, `F`
quasi-coherent, and `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X`, an isomorphism in the weak
existence form `Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F)` (under
`[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`), where `higherDirectImage f i F =
((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F`. End-state: zero inline `sorry` in the cone, zero
project axioms, kernel-only axioms. Extraction from the Algebraic-Jacobian challenge; the
downstream Picard/Quot machinery is out of scope and was carved away.

## Phases & estimations

| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P3 affine acyclicity (`CechAcyclic.affine`) — the long pole | ACTIVE (statement-gap fix first) | ~4–7 | ~250–550 | from-scratch: standard-cover Čech complex = complex of localisations; prime-local contracting homotopy `h(s)_{i₀…iₚ}=s_{i_fix i₀…iₚ}`; `isZero` via localise-at-prime. Mathlib LACKS all of these for `Scheme.Modules` (leansearch confirms only generic `Acyclic`/`ExactAt`). | Statement gap (Open Q): blueprint proves STANDARD-cover; Lean sig takes general `X.OpenCover`. DECIDED: narrow non-protected signature to standard covers (downstream-safe via P5a basis lemma). Needs a "standard affine cover" Lean type (design — analogist consult). Then build localisation Čech + homotopy from scratch. Every geometric node routes through this except `lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf`. |
| P5a vanishing inputs (mostly P3-dependent; one P3-independent leaf) | NEXT | ~3–6 | ~250–550 | augmented-Čech-is-a-resolution (`cechAugmented_exact`); presheaf description `R^if_*=sheafify(V↦H^i(f⁻¹V))` (`higher_direct_image_presheaf`, Stacks 01XJ); basis lemma `lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis`; affine Serre vanishing | All P5a decls ABSENT from Lean (scaffold first). `lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf` is the lone P3-independent leaf, BUT itself needs the rightDerived↔sheafified-presheaf-cohomology comparison for `Scheme.Modules` — Mathlib's version is `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`, wrong category, so also from-scratch. `cechAugmented_exact`/basis/serre all consume narrowed-P3. Statement↔proof parity on basis lemma still open (iter-009 writer note). |
| P5b comparison assembly | LAST (needs P3, P4, P5a) | ~2–4 | ~150–300 | P3 + P4 + P5a + termwise `f_*`-acyclicity of `Cᵖ` (relative affine vanishing after localising to affine `S`) | Final assembly of `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (protected, frozen sig+path) from the resolution (P5a), termwise acyclicity (`cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic` via P3 + relative reduction), and the P4 acyclic-resolution comparison (now an off-the-shelf engine). Routes the general finite-affine `𝒰` through the basis lemma, NOT `CechAcyclic.affine` directly. Lean proof comment still describes the OLD spectral-sequence route — clean during a refactor. |

## Completed

| Phase | Iters (done@ · used) | LOC | Files | Key results | Reusable techniques | Pitfalls |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 push–pull functor laws | 002 · 2 | ~120 | `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` | `pushPullMap_comp` (composition law) closing the push–pull functoriality cone | object-form alignment `simp only [Functor.comp_obj]` BEFORE `reassoc_of%`; `rawPushPullMap` + `subst`-the-over-triangles + pentagon, sidestepping `conjugateEquiv` | the `conjugateEquiv_comp` mate route is INFEASIBLE (kernel `whnf` blow-up); do not retry it |
| P2 `CechNerve`/`CechComplex` | 002 · 1 (stretch) | ~60 | `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` | `pushPullFunctor` (G), `coverCechNerveOver(Aug)`, `CechNerve`, `CechComplex` all axiom-clean | once `G` is a functor: `Over.lift` + `.rightOp` + `CosimplicialObject.Augmented.whiskeringObj`; terminal-object augmentation (`Over.mkIdTerminal`) makes coherence automatic | none — clean off-the-shelf transport |
| P4 abstract acyclic-resolution lemma (Leray's acyclicity, Stacks 015E) | 009 · 6 (004–009) | ~965 (file) | `AcyclicResolution.lean` | `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` (RⁿG ≅ Hⁿ(G K•) for a G-acyclic resolution), `rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic`, horseshoe `ofShortExact`, object-level dimension shift, cosyzygy layer — all axiom-clean | decompose-then-build cadence (effort-break → frontier-leaf → assembly); two-step `cokernel.mapIso` for non-syntactic homology naturality; `isLimitForkMapOfIsLimit'`+`conePointUniqueUpToIso` for left-exact transport; `Nat.rec` staircase off a generalized `stairGen` | `ShortComplex.mapCyclesIso` is WRONG for a left-exact functor (needs preserve-colimit); `← G.map_comp` silently fails beside a mapped-complex term (isolate in a clean `have`, term mode) |

## Routes

### Route A — acyclic-resolution comparison (CHOSEN)
The augmented Čech complex `0 → F → C⁰ → C¹ → ⋯` on `X` (`Cᵖ = ∏ (j_s)_*(F|_{U_s})` over
`(p+1)`-fold intersections) is (i) a resolution of `F` and (ii) termwise `(pushforward f)`-
acyclic, because each intersection is affine (relative Serre vanishing, P3). The abstract
homological-algebra theorem "a `G`-acyclic resolution computes `G.rightDerived`" (P4, built
by dimension-shifting from Mathlib's `InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj`) then gives
`Hⁱ(f_* C•) = Hⁱ(CechComplex) ≅ (pushforward f).rightDerived i F` directly — ONE abstract
lemma, NO spectral sequences. This is the standard Cartan–Leray acyclic-cover proof of the
existence statement; it folds P3 in as its acyclicity input.

### Route B — two spectral sequences (REJECTED, fallback only)
The literal Stacks 02KE route: a Čech-to-derived spectral sequence plus the Leray spectral
sequence for `Scheme.Modules`. Rejected: both spectral sequences are absent from Mathlib
(multi-thousand-LOC to build), and the Leray degeneration additionally needs quasi-coherence
of `R^q f_* F` (`lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images`, itself non-trivial via relative
Mayer–Vietoris). Strictly heavier than Route A for the same `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` goal. Kept on
record only as a fallback if Route A's abstract lemma proves unexpectedly hard.

### The P3-narrowing ↔ P5a-basis-lemma bridge (load-bearing)
Narrowing the non-protected `CechAcyclic.affine` signature to STANDARD covers is
downstream-safe **only** because the final assembly (P5b) does not apply `CechAcyclic.affine`
to the general finite-affine `𝒰` directly. Instead, the general-cover intersection vanishing
`H^q(U_σ, F) = 0` is obtained from narrowed standard-cover acyclicity through the basis lemma
`lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis` (Stacks 01EO): standard affine covers `D(f_i)` form a basis,
and 01EO lifts Čech-acyclicity on a basis to sheaf-cohomology vanishing on every affine open.
Hence the basis lemma (scoped in P5a) is the linchpin connecting narrowed P3 to the general
`𝒰` in the frozen goal; "downstream-safe" is a *derived* claim contingent on building it.
The project does NOT formalize the full Stacks-01EO bootstrap: it only needs the affine /
standard-cover special case, proved directly via the P4 acyclic-resolution theorem applied to
the augmented Čech complex with `G = Γ(B,-)` (term-acyclicity from the P3 contracting homotopy).
See Open strategic questions for the enumerated reduction.

## Open strategic questions

- P5a basis lemma `lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis`: RESOLVED scoping (iter-009). Mathlib's
  site cohomology (`Sites/SheafCohomology/*`) is `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`, the WRONG category
  (not `O_X`-modules) — not directly reusable. DECIDED route: do NOT formalize the general
  Stacks-01EO bootstrap. The project only ever applies the basis lemma to AFFINE opens
  with STANDARD covers, where the reduced-scope rewrite proves it directly: apply
  `lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived` with `G = Γ(B,-)` to the augmented Čech complex of a
  standard cover, term-acyclicity from the same prime-local contracting homotopy as
  `lem:cech_acyclic_affine`. So the basis lemma `\uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine,
  lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}` — it consumes narrowed P3 (non-circular).
- P3 statement gap: narrow `CechAcyclic.affine` to a standard-cover hypothesis (DECIDED). Open
  sub-question: the precise "standard affine cover" Lean type (non-trivial design); resolve in
  the P3 refactor before the effort-break.
- P3: exact Mathlib names for prime-local exactness; verify before dispatching.
- P5b: termwise `(pushforward f)`-acyclicity reduces to affine Serre vanishing via Stacks
  `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing` after localising to affine `S` (resolved).
- P5a/P5b blueprint Route-A-clean rewrite: DONE iter-009. Pending: fresh whole-blueprint gate
  review (this iter); statement↔proof parity on `lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis`.
- File-split for parallelism: all P5 decls are absent from Lean and live under ONE consolidated
  chapter / one file, so they cannot be parallel prover lanes as-is. DECIDE the multi-file
  decomposition and scaffold each into its own file under the same `AlgebraicGeometry` namespace +
  the consolidated chapter's `% archon:covers`. Keep `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` sig+path frozen.

## Mathlib gaps & new material

Gaps to fill (project-side, Mathlib lacks):
- Standard-cover Čech complex as the complex of localisations + its prime-local contracting
  homotopy / positive-degree exactness (P3).
- Relative affine vanishing `R^i(affine morphism)_* = 0 (i>0)` and `R^q(jₛ)_* = 0` for affine
  open immersions into separated `X`, for `Scheme.Modules` (P5b termwise acyclicity / P5a).
- Flat pushforward preserves injective `O_X`-modules — backs the SS-free open-immersion rewrite.
- Augmented Čech complex is a resolution (stalkwise exactness) for `Scheme.Modules` (P5a).
- Čech-to-cohomology-on-a-basis (P5a) — NOT atomic. Project-only special case reduces to
  `lem:cech_acyclic_affine` + the P4 acyclic-resolution theorem with `G = Γ(B,-)`.

New project material:
- push–pull functor `G : (Over X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ X.Modules`, `p ↦ p_* p^* F` — keep hand-rolled.
- `CechNerve` / `CechComplex` / `CechAcyclic.affine`.
- `AcyclicResolution.lean`: abstract acyclic-resolution comparison.
- comparison assembly `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` via Route A.

# Mathlib Analogist Directive

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
p3design

## Design question

How should the P3 affine-acyclicity lemma `CechAcyclic.affine` be (re-)signed and built in Lean, given that its proof is the *standard-cover* prime-local contracting-homotopy argument? Two coupled decisions:

1. **The "standard affine open cover" Lean type.** The blueprint proves vanishing only for a *standard* open cover `U = ⋃ D(fᵢ)` of an affine `U = Spec A` (the `fᵢ ∈ A` generating the unit ideal), but the current Lean signature takes a general `𝒰 : X.OpenCover` (see `## Project artifact(s)`). We have DECIDED to narrow the non-protected signature to standard covers. What is the right Lean encoding of "a standard affine open cover of an affine scheme"? Options to weigh: (a) a bundled structure carrying `Fin n → Γ(X, O_X)` + a `Ideal.span = ⊤` proof + the identification of the cover opens with `basicOpen fᵢ`; (b) a predicate on an existing `X.OpenCover`; (c) reuse of an existing Mathlib notion (e.g. `PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen`, `IsAffineOpen`, `Scheme.affineCover`, the `Spec`/`basicOpen` API, or any "covers by basic opens / generate unit ideal" idiom). Does Mathlib already have a standard-affine-cover construct or the closest idiom?

2. **The localisation Čech complex + its exactness.** The mathematical core is: for `M` an `A`-module and `f₁,…,fₙ` generating `(1)`, the extended complex `0 → M → ∏ M_{f_{i₀}} → ∏ M_{f_{i₀}f_{i₁}} → ⋯` is exact, proved by localising at each prime `𝔭`, picking `i_fix` with `f_{i_fix} ∉ 𝔭`, and using the contracting homotopy `h(s)_{i₀…iₚ} = s_{i_fix i₀…iₚ}`. Does Mathlib already have ANY form of this — the algebraic standard-cover Čech exactness (Stacks `algebra-lemma-cover-module` / `algebra-lemma-cover-module-exact`), a Koszul/Čech complex of localisations, or `exact-after-localisation` machinery (`Module.eq_zero_of_localization`, `LocalizedModule`, faithfully-flat-cover exactness)? What is the right Mathlib idiom for "a complex of modules is exact ⇔ exact after localising at every prime", and for assembling the alternating-sum localisation complex? Identify the building blocks so the from-scratch build aligns with Mathlib rather than re-inventing a parallel API.

## Project artifact(s) under question
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean`:764-774 — `theorem CechAcyclic.affine [IsAffine X] (f : X ⟶ S) [IsAffineHom f] (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀] (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (p : ℕ) (hp : 1 ≤ p) : IsZero ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology p)` — currently `sorry`; general `X.OpenCover`, to be narrowed to standard covers.
- `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`: blocks `def:cech_complex`, `lem:cech_acyclic_affine` (the proof sketch with the prime-local contracting homotopy), and `lem:cech_augmented_resolution` (the extended-complex exactness consumer).

## Why now

P3 is the project's long-pole bottleneck (every geometric node routes through `CechAcyclic.affine`). Before scaffolding the narrowed signature + the from-scratch localisation-homotopy infrastructure, I want the Lean encoding of "standard affine cover" and the localisation-Čech-exactness building blocks to be Mathlib-aligned, so the ~250–550 LOC we build composes with Mathlib (`PrimeSpectrum`, `LocalizedModule`, `IsLocalization`, the `Spec`/`O_X.Modules` API) rather than forming a parallel API. The narrowed signature will be the input type the prover formalizes against; getting its shape right now avoids a costly re-sign later.

## Hints
Relevant Mathlib namespaces to probe: `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.OpenCover`, `AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen`, `PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen`, `RingedSpace`/`Scheme.basicOpen`, `Ideal.span`/`Submodule.span ... = ⊤`, `IsLocalization`, `LocalizedModule`, `Module.localization`, `Localization.away`, exactness-via-localisation (`Module.eq_zero_iff_forall_isLocalizedModule` / `LinearMap.exact` localisation lemmas), `CategoryTheory.ShortComplex` / `HomologicalComplex` over `ModuleCat`. Stacks tags: `algebra-lemma-cover-module`, `algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local`. Also check whether `SheafOfModules` / `Scheme.Modules` has a section-over-affine = module-localisation bridge (`IsAffineOpen.…` global-sections-as-localisation lemmas).

## Severity expectation
high-stakes — this design is load-bearing for the entire P3/P5 geometric build; be strict about idiom adherence and name the concrete cost of any parallel-API path.

mapped-complex term (a `HasHomology`-diamond reducibility quirk) — the entire 23:04–23:18 burst of

`map_comp` rewrite on a clean `have` and close in term mode. Both are now in the Knowledge Base.
