573	\begin{lemma}
574	[Serre vanishing on affines]
575	  \label{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}
576	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_serre_vanishing}
577	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}
578	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG, Lemma
579	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on
580	  % affines) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L145--155).
581	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
582	  % for quasi-coherent modules.
583	  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
584	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
585	  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
586	  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
587	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG
588	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
589	  affines).}
590	  Let \(U\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
591	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. Then the sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\) vanishes in
592	  all positive degrees:
593	  \[
594	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0.
595	  \]
596	\end{lemma}

598	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We are going to apply
599	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.

601	% the affine opens of $X$.
602	% As our set $\text{Cov}$ of open coverings we are going to use the standard
603	% open coverings of affine opens of $X$.
604	% Next we check that conditions (1), (2) and (3) of
605	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
606	% hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
607	% another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
608	% The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
609	% of $\mathcal{B}$, see
610	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.

612	% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from

616	  Take the affine opens of \(U\) as a basis \(\mathcal{B}\) of the topology and the
617	  standard open covers of affine opens as the admissible covers. The intersection of
618	  two standard opens in an affine is again a standard open, so \(\mathcal{B}\) is
619	  closed under the relevant intersections; and the standard covers form a cofinal
620	  system among the open covers of any basic open. The standard-cover {\v C}ech
621	  vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} supplies the acyclicity
622	  hypothesis. The {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis
623	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) then identifies the sheaf cohomology
624	  of \(\mathcal{F}\) on each basic open with its {\v C}ech cohomology for a standard
625	  cover, whence \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
626	\end{proof}

629	[{\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis]

665	  % hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is

673	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Lemma
674	  \texttt{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (as applied in the proof of
675	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}).}
676	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\mathcal{B}\) be a basis for its topology, and let a
677	  set of admissible open covers be given, subject to: (1) \(\mathcal{B}\) is closed
678	  under finite intersection; (2) for each \(B \in \mathcal{B}\) the admissible covers
679	  of \(B\) are cofinal among all open covers of \(B\); and (3) for every admissible
680	  cover \(\mathcal{U}\) of every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\), every quasi-coherent
681	  \(\mathcal{F}\), and every \(p > 0\), the {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes,
682	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Then for every \(B \in \mathcal{B}\)
683	  and every \(p\) the canonical comparison map is an isomorphism
684	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong H^p(B, \mathcal{F})\); in particular,
685	  under (3), \(H^p(B, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). This is the

688	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}.

691	\begin{proof}
692	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
693	  Take \(X\) a scheme, \(\mathcal{B}\) the basis of affine opens, the admissible
694	  covers the standard affine covers, and \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Fix an
695	  affine open \(B = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \in \mathcal{B}\) and a standard cover
696	  \(\mathcal{U} : B = \bigcup_i D(f_i)\); we show \(H^k(B, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all
697	  \(k \geq 1\) and that the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison is an isomorphism,
698	  via the acyclic-resolution route.
699	
700	  Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex of \(\mathcal{U}\) on \(B\),
701	  \[
702	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\; \cdots,
703	    \qquad
704	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} (j_\sigma)_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_\sigma}\bigr),
705	  \]
706	  the product running over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections \(U_\sigma =
707	  D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\). By
708	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} this augmented complex is exact, so
709	  \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\); its exactness is the
710	  prime-local contracting homotopy of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, with no
711	  sheaf-cohomology input.
712	
713	  Each term \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is acyclic for the global-sections functor
714	  \(G = \Gamma(B, -)\). Indeed each \(U_\sigma = D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\) is a basic
715	  open of \(B\), hence affine, and the higher cohomology of
716	  \((j_\sigma)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_\sigma})\) on \(B\) is computed by the module-level
717	  {\v C}ech complex of a standard cover of the affine \(U_\sigma\), which is exact in
718	  positive degrees by the same contracting homotopy of
719	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} applied one step down to \(U_\sigma\); a product
720	  of \(G\)-acyclic objects is \(G\)-acyclic. There is no spectral sequence and no
721	  appeal to sheaf cohomology — only standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing.
722

843	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
844	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
845	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
846	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\); since the abelian
847	  structure on \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules computes cokernels and kernels objectwise on
848	  the level of presheaves and then sheafifies, that cohomology sheaf is the
849	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)

851	  the last equality because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is an injective (hence
852	  acyclic) resolution computing the absolute cohomology of \(f^{-1}(V)\). Evaluation
853	  of a sheafification vanishes exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis,
854	  giving the stated affine-local criterion.

919	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), (j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr)
920	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
921	  \]
922	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
923	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
924	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
925	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
926	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
927	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing
928	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} the cohomology
929	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
930	  Since the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), this forces
931	  \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\).
932	
933	  Thus \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an \(f_*\)-acyclic resolution of
934	  \(j_*\mathcal{H}\), and the acyclic-resolution comparison
935	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} applied with \(G = f_*\) gives,
936	  for every \(k\),
937	  \[
938	    R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)
939	      \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)
940	      \;=\; H^k\bigl((f \circ j)_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\bigr)
941	      \;=\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H},
942	  \]
943	  the middle equality because \(f_* \circ j_* = (f \circ j)_* = g_*\), and the last
944	  because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(\mathcal{H}\)
945	  computing \(R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\).
946	\end{proof}
947	
948	\begin{lemma}
949	[Each {\v C}ech term is $f_*$-acyclic]
950	  \label{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}
951	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic}
952	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
953	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
954	  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct
955	  % images for affine morphisms) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
956	  % L180--199).
957	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
958	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
959	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
960	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
961	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative affine vanishing).}
962	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
963	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\mathfrak{U}\) a finite affine open
964	  cover of \(X\) with all intersections affine. Then for every \(p \geq 0\) the
965	  degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech term \(\mathcal{C}^p =
966	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\)
967	  is right \((\,\cdot\,)_*\)-acyclic for the pushforward \(f_*\):
968	  \[
969	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0
970	      \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
971	  \]
972	\end{lemma}
973	
974	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
975	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
976	% the sheaf
977	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
978	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
979	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
980	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
981	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
982	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
983	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
984	% opens we win."
985	\begin{proof}
986	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
987	  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
988	  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
989	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
990	  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
991	  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
992	  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
993	  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
994	  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
995	  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
996	  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
997	  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).
998	
999	  Now decompose the structure map \(g_s := f \circ j_s : U_s \to S\). First,
1000	  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
1001	  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
1002	  \(q \geq 1\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} part (2),
1003	  \(R^k f_*\bigl((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\bigr) \cong
1004	  R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\). Second, working locally over an affine
1005	  \(V \subseteq S\) as above, \(g_s\) restricts to a morphism of affine schemes
1006	  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
1007	  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
1008	  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing
1009	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): \(R^k(g_s)_*\mathcal{G}\) is the
1010	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
1011	  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
1012	  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
1013	  \(k \geq 1\).
1014	\end{proof}
1015	
1016	\begin{lemma}
1017	
1018	\leanok
1019	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
1020	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
1021	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
1022	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
1023	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
1024	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
1025	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
1026	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
1027	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
1028	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
1029	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
1030	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
1031	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
1032	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
1033	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
1034	  % $$
1035	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
1036	  % $$
1037	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
1038	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
1039	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
1040	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
1041	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
1042	  % we have
1043	  % $$
1044	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
1045	  % $$
1046	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
1047	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
1048	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
1049	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
1050	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
1051	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
1052	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
1053	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
1054	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
1055	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1056	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
1057	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
1058	  \[
1059	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
1060	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
1061	  \]
1062	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
1063	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
1064	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
1065	
1066	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
1067	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
1068	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
1069	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
1070	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
1071	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
1072	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
1073	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
1074	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
1075	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
1076	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
1077	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
1078	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
1079	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
1080	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
1081	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
1082	  role in the applications.
1083	\end{lemma}
1084	
1085	\begin{proof}
1086	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
1087	  Apply the abstract Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with the
1088	  right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
1089	  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
1090	  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
1091	  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. The two hypotheses of the
1092	  abstract lemma are exactly the geometric sub-lemmas established above:
1093	  \begin{enumerate}
1094	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
1095	      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
1096	      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
1097	      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
1098	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
1099	      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
1100	      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
1101	  \end{enumerate}
1102	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
1103	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
1104	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
1105	  resolution,
1106	  \[
1107	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
1108	      \;\cong\;
1109	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
1110	  \]
1111	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
1112	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
1113	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
1114	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
1115	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
1116	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
1117	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
1118	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
1119	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
1120	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
1121	  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
1122	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
1123	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).
1124	\end{proof}

input.description: Show CechAcyclic.affine and main theorem signatures

content:
localising at an arbitrary prime `𝔭`; choosing `i_fix` with `f_{i_fix} ∉ 𝔭`, the
prescription `h(s)_{i₀…i_p} = s_{i_fix i₀…i_p}` is a contracting homotopy, so the
localised complex is exact, hence so is the complex. The Čech-to-cohomology
comparison on the basis of affine opens then gives the sheaf statement. -/
theorem CechAcyclic.affine [IsAffine X] (f : X ⟶ S) [IsAffineHom f]
    (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (p : ℕ) (hp : 1 ≤ p) :
    IsZero ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology p) := by
  -- Proof (Stacks 02KG): on the affine `X = Spec A` the Čech complex of the
  -- standard cover is the complex of localisations; positive-degree exactness
  -- follows from the prime-local contracting homotopy `h(s)_{i₀…i_p} =
  -- s_{i_fix i₀…i_p}` (where `f_{i_fix} ∉ 𝔭`), giving `(dh + hd) = id`. Needs the
  -- explicit localisation description of `CechComplex` on affines and the
  -- module-level homotopy, currently absent from Mathlib for `Scheme.Modules`.
  sorry

/-- **The Čech complex computes the higher direct images** (Stacks 02KE;
`lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent` and
`lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application`).

Let `f : X ⟶ S` be separated and quasi-compact, `F` a quasi-coherent
`O_X`-module, and `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X` (so, by separatedness,
every intersection is affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative Čech
complex compute the higher direct images: for every `i ≥ 0` there is a canonical
isomorphism of `O_S`-modules
```
  Hⁱ(Č•(𝒰, F)) ≅ Rⁱ f_* F.
```
In particular, over an affine base `S = Spec A`, taking global sections gives
`Hⁱ(X, F) = Čⁱ(𝒰, F) = H⁰(S, Rⁱ f_* F)` as `A`-modules.

We state the isomorphism as `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` and compare against the
derived-functor higher direct image `higherDirectImage` wherever the latter is
defined (`HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules`).

The proof (Stacks 02KE): the question is local on `S`, reducing to `S` affine; by
affine acyclicity (`CechAcyclic.affine`) the higher cohomology of `F` over each
affine intersection vanishes, so the Čech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
collapses to its `q = 0` row, identifying Čech cohomology with sheaf cohomology;
over affine `S` the Leray spectral sequence then degenerates (Serre vanishing for
the quasi-coherent `Rⁱ f_* F`), giving the stated isomorphism. -/
theorem cech_computes_higherDirectImage [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
    (f : X ⟶ S) [QuasiCompact f] [IsSeparated f] (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀]
    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (i : ℕ) :
    Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F) := by
  -- Proof (Stacks 02KE): reduce to `S` affine; affine acyclicity
  -- (`CechAcyclic.affine`) collapses the Čech-to-cohomology spectral sequence to
  -- its `q = 0` row, and the Leray spectral sequence degenerates by Serre
  -- vanishing for the quasi-coherent `Rⁱ f_* F`, yielding the comparison iso.
  -- Needs the two spectral sequences for `Scheme.Modules`, currently absent from
  -- Mathlib.
  sorry

108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** — the `\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:
130	  - **Broken edges** — `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.
131	  - **Missing edges** — read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as "broken"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.
132	  - **Isolated declarations** — `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight — so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate "needs a blueprint entry" signal, not removal candidates.
133	- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)** — it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\mathlibok` (with `\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* — never flag them as ∞ holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide — or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's — is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is "add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it," not "invent a project lemma."
134	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
135	- **Rendering integrity (run `archon blueprint-doctor`)** — the blueprint must *render*: as the compiled leanblueprint site and in the dashboard. The doctor lints this deterministically — run `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once and triage every `malformed_refs` finding into your report:
136	  - **`undefined-macro`** — a chapter uses `\foo` that no `macros/*.tex` or chapter-local `\providecommand` defines: it renders as raw TeX everywhere. Recommend the definition (usually obvious from context: `\Z` → `\mathbb{Z}`, `\fppf` → `\mathrm{fppf}`); when the intent is genuinely unclear (you cannot tell what the writer meant), flag it as a correctness finding for the chapter's writer instead of guessing.
137	  - **`math-delim`** — interleaved `$ … \( … \) … $` delimiters that shred formulas mid-sentence; per-line locations are in the finding. Must-fix, writer-directive material.
138	  - **`literal-ref` / `bare-label`** — placeholder "REF" tokens and raw label ids in prose; each needs a `\cref{}` or the human-readable number.
139	  Group these per chapter in your findings so the dispatcher can scope one writer per damaged chapter.
140	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
141	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
142	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
143	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
144	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
145	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
146	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
147	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
148	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
149	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
150	
151	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
152	
153	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
154	
155	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
156	
157	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
158	
159	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
160	
161	## Always read everything
162	
163	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
164	
165	## Unstarted-phase proposals
166	
167	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
168	
169	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. (That table lists only *remaining* phases — finished ones live in `## Completed` and need no proposal.) For each remaining phase row whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
170	
171	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
172	
173	The proposal answers:
174	
175	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
176	
177	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
178	
179	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
180	
181	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
182	
183	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
184	
185	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
186	
187	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
188	
189	```
190	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
191	
192	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
193	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
194	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
195	
196	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
197	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
198	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
199	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
200	...
201	
202	**`\uses` skeleton**:
203	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
204	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
205	
206	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
207	
208	**References for writer**:
209	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
210	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
211	
212	**Subphase choices exposed**:
213	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
214	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
215	```
216	
217	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
218	
219	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
220	
221	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
222	
223	## Directive Format
224	
225	```markdown
226	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
227	
228	## Slug
229	<slug>
230	
231	## Strategy snapshot
232	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
233	
234	## Routes
235	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
236	
237	## References
238	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
239	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
240	
241	## Focus areas (optional)
242	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
243	
244	## Known issues
245	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
246	```
247	
248	## What you do
249	
250	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
251	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
252	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
253	   - Read the entire chapter.
254	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
255	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
256	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
257	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
258	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
259	6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:
260	   - **wire-up** — it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding — recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.
261	   - **remove** — it is genuinely orphaned scaffolding: nothing in the goal's proof closure needs it, it isn't the goal, and it isn't a `\mathlibok`/reference anchor. Only then is removal the right call. Name the chapter + label so the plan agent can authorize a writer to delete it.
262	   - **keep** — it is intentionally standalone (the goal theorem with nothing above it, a deliberately isolated `\mathlibok` reference, etc.). Say why and move on.
263	   You **flag and recommend**; you never edit. The plan agent turns a `wire-up`/`remove` disposition into a blueprint-writer directive.
264	7. **Run the rendering lint.** `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once for the whole blueprint; triage every `malformed_refs` finding (`undefined-macro`, `math-delim`, `literal-ref`, `bare-label`) per the "Rendering integrity" bullet above, grouped per chapter.
265	8. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
266	9. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
267	10. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
268	
269	You may also use:
270	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
271	- **`archon blueprint-doctor [--json]`** — the deterministic rendering/structure lint (orphan chapters, broken refs, literal-REF, interleaved math delimiters, bare labels, undefined macros, covers problems). Read-only; run it once per review and triage its `malformed_refs` (see "Rendering integrity" above).
272	- **`leandag`** — your read-only window into the real dependency DAG. Run it freely: it never touches project source or the blueprint. (`leandag build` refreshes the derived `.leandag/` cache, which is regenerated deterministically from the current tree — that is a tool artifact, not a project edit, so it does not violate your read-only rule.)
273	  - `leandag build --json` — the structured build report: `unknown_uses` (broken `\uses{}`), `unmatched_lean` (`\lean{}` pointing nowhere), `conflicts`, and `summary.isolated`.
274	  - `leandag stats` — counts including `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint`.
275	  - `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated [--type theorem] [--chapter <c>]`) — the isolated declarations, which you triage per node (below).
276	  - `leandag show gaps` — blueprint nodes missing a `\lean{}`.
277	
278	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
279	
280	## Report format
281	
282	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
283	
284	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
285	
286	Concretely:
287	
288	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline`, `### Dependency & isolation findings` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all six are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
289	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
290	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
291	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
292	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
293	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
294	
295	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
296	
297	```markdown
298	# Blueprint Review Report
299	
300	## Slug
301	<slug>
302	
303	## Iteration
304	<NNN>
305	
306	## Top-level summaries
307	
308	### Incomplete parts
309	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
310	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
311	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
312	
313	### Proofs lacking detail
314	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
315	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
316	
317	### Lean difficulty quality
318	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
319	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
320	
321	### Multi-route coverage
322	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
323	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
324	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
325	
326	### Citation discipline
327	<bullets naming chapter + label + which citation element is missing or suspect. Omit when zero findings.>
328	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: `% SOURCE:` line has no `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical — writer did not name the local file. Likely fabrication.
329	- `Bar.tex` / `\def:scheme`: `% SOURCE:` claims `(read from references/hartshorne-II-1.md)` but that file does not exist on disk. Fabrication; writer must dispatch a retriever or remove the citation.
330	
331	### Dependency & isolation findings
332	<from the leandag audit. Each line: chapter / label — finding + disposition the plan agent should hand a writer. Omit when zero findings.>
333	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: `\uses{}` is missing `lem:bar` (the proof's "by the gluing lemma" step depends on it). **wire-up** — add `\uses{lem:bar}`.
334	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:foo` → `\lem:gone`: broken `\uses{}` (no declaration defines `lem:gone`). **fix** — correct the label or add the missing lemma.
335	- `Aux.tex` / `\lem:orphan`: isolated (no `\uses{}` out, nothing uses it), not the goal, not `\mathlibok`, nothing in the goal closure needs it. **remove** — authorize a writer to delete it from `Aux.tex`.
336	- `Main.tex` / `\thm:goal`: isolated but it is the project goal. **keep**.
337	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:foo` → `\lem:hensel`: broken `\uses{}`, and `lem:hensel` is a Mathlib result. **wire-up** — add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it (state it, `\lean{Mathlib...}`), not a project lemma.
338	- `Bar.tex` / `\lem:claimed_mathlib`: marked `\mathlibok` but Mathlib has no such declaration / the stated form is stronger than Mathlib's. **fix** — hard fail; the loop would skip a real gap. Remove the marker and treat as a result to prove, or correct the statement to match Mathlib.
339

341	
342	<One proposal block per strategy phase with no or stub blueprint coverage. Each block is a complete chapter outline as specified in "Unstarted-phase proposals" above — not a one-line flag. Omit this section only when every phase has adequate coverage.>

352	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
353	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
354

356	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
357	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
358

360

362	- `references/<slug>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
363	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name>">

369	
370	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
371	- **complete**: true | partial | false
372	- **correct**: true | partial | false
373	- **notes**:
374	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
375	  - ...
376

378	
379	(Cover every chapter. Chapters with findings get the full multi-line
380	block. Chapters that are `complete: true`, `correct: true`, with
381	no notes worth surfacing get the compact one-liner.)
382

384	
385	- `<chapter A>` defines `\foo` but `<chapter B>`'s proof of `\bar` uses a stronger version.
386	- ...
387	
388	(Omit if empty.)
389	
390	## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)
391	
392	If you found a definition that, on close reading, conflicts with the strategy in a way that requires a strategy change (not just a chapter rewrite), name it here.
393	
394	- `<chapter A>` / `\def:foo`: as currently defined, this <conflict>. STRATEGY.md says <X>, but `foo` actually does <Y>.
395	
396	## Severity summary
397	
398	Apply these rules verbatim:
399	
400	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
401	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
402	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
403	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter.
404	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
405	  - **Blueprint purity violations** — any chapter containing Lean syntax or project history must be cleaned before provers run.
406	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels.
407	  - **Missing `\uses{}` edges** (a `wire-up` disposition) on a chapter feeding an active prover route — out-of-order dispatch risk.
408	  - **Unfaithful `\mathlibok` anchors** — a `\mathlibok` block whose `\lean{}` Mathlib declaration doesn't exist or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's. Like a fabricated citation, this hides a real gap from the loop; always must-fix.
409	  - **Citation-discipline findings on blocks feeding an active prover route**.
410	  - **Unstarted-phase proposals** — each proposed chapter lands here as `unstarted-phase proposal: <phase name> — dispatch blueprint-writer for <proposed chapter filename> or record deferral`. These are must-act because an unwritten blueprint is an unparallelisable, unpreviewable phase; writing it early is the cheapest action in the loop.
411	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:
412	  - Lean-difficulty-quality findings for hints NOT in an active prover route.
413	  - Citation-discipline findings on blocks NOT feeding an active prover route.
414	  - Missing-edge `wire-up` findings on chapters NOT feeding an active prover route, and every `remove` disposition (orphaned-scaffolding cleanup is never urgent).
415	  - Informational cross-chapter style issues.
416	- **informational** — minor observations: naming drift, optional `\lean{...}` references to helpers worth promoting, low-impact prose suggestions.
417	
418	Overall verdict: one sentence. If unstarted-phase proposals exist, the verdict must name them — "N phases have no blueprint coverage; proposals provided for immediate writer dispatch" is a required component of the verdict when N > 0.
419	```
420	
421	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter. Unstarted-phase proposals do not block existing provers — they trigger blueprint-writer dispatch for future prover work. Do not under-classify them as "informational"; the project pays in deferred parallelism every iter a phase sits without a blueprint.
422	
423	## Return value
424	
425	Your final assistant message:
426	
427	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> chapters audited, <M> findings, <K> unstarted-phase proposals`
428	- Top-level summary counts (incomplete parts, proofs lacking detail, unstarted phases, etc.)
429	- The path to your full report.
430	
431	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
432	
433	## Reminders
434	
435	- **Read every chapter, no scope shortcuts.**
436	- **Cross-reference every strategy phase against existing chapters.** An unstarted phase is not a clean bill of health — it is an opportunity to write the blueprint now and enable parallelism.
437	- **Proposals are actionable, not informational.** Each proposal block must be concrete enough to serve as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Vague "chapter needed for X" notes are not proposals.
438	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
439	- **You audit against the directive's context**, not your own ideas of what the project should look like — but you ARE critical of weak prose and under-specified Lean hints.
440	- **You flag, you don't fix.** Even when the fix is obvious, the plan agent decides what changes next iter.
441	- **The per-chapter shape is fixed**: `complete`, `correct`, `notes`. Don't reshape. The plan agent depends on this format.changes next iter.
442	- **The per-chapter shape is fixed**: `complete`, `correct`, `notes`. Don't reshape. The plan agent depends on this format. this format.

61	
62	# Strategy Critic
63	
64	You are the fresh-context strategy critic. You read the project's `STRATEGY.md` with **no exposure to its iter-by-iter history** and challenge the strategic choices as if a mathematician encountering this project for the first time would.
65	
66	Your job is to be the project's adversarial reader. The plan agent has been in the project's context for many iterations and is naturally invested in the existing routes. You are the corrective.
67	
68	## Your context discipline
69	
70	Your directive will name ONLY:
71	
72	- The current `STRATEGY.md` (the primary subject of your review).
73	- The project's stated final goal (one paragraph naming the theorem(s) to formalize).
74	- A short reference index (`references/summary.md` content).
75	- A blueprint summary (chapter titles + one-line topic per chapter).
76	
77	Your directive will NOT include `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, prover task results, review summaries, or recent narrative. **If the directive accidentally includes any of these, ignore them.** Your value depends entirely on the lack of pollution. Reading the iter-by-iter history would make you the planner; the planner already exists. Stay fresh.
78	
79	You may use:
80	
81	- The references named in the directive (read them if needed for the math).
82	- The blueprint chapter summaries (the prose summary in the directive, NOT the full chapter text — full-chapter audit is the territory of the blueprint-review subagent in the catalog).
83	- `archon-lean-lsp` if you need to spot-check a Mathlib name's existence.
84	
85	You may NOT use:
86	
87	- Iter sidecars, even if you find their paths.
88	- Any state file other than what the directive named.
89	
90	## What you check
91	
92	For each strategic route in `STRATEGY.md`:
93	
94	1. **Goal-alignment.** Does this route, if executed, actually produce the project's stated final goal? Or is there a subtle gap where the route's end-state isn't quite what the goal demands?
95	
96	2. **Mathematical soundness.** Is the route's mathematical argument plausible? Are there steps that look like they assume something the route's prerequisites don't give you? A fresh reader is good at spotting "wait, this step needs X but X isn't established."
97	
98	3. **Alternative routes.** Are there other approaches the strategy doesn't mention? For each, briefly note: what it would look like, why it might be cheaper or sounder, and why the strategy may have rejected it (if you can tell from prose).
99	
100	4. **Sunk-cost reasoning.** Does the strategy justify a route in terms of "we've already done X" rather than "X is the right way"? That's a sunk-cost smell. Call it out by name.
101	
102	5. **Prerequisite assumptions.** Does the strategy assume Mathlib infrastructure that may not exist? Verify the named lemmas / type classes / structures actually exist (use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for spot-checks). Strategy that depends on phantom Mathlib infra is invalid.
103	
104	6. **Effort estimates.** If the strategy carries per-route LOC or iteration estimates, do they look honest given the scope of the route? Estimates that are wildly under-counted (e.g. "200 LOC for representability of Pic") indicate either underestimated effort or a misplanned route. The LOC cell is a rough remaining-LOC range (e.g. `~80–220`); flag rows whose LOC range is grossly inconsistent with `Iters left` given the route's scope — e.g. a sprawling representability phase marked `~30` LOC with `Iters left: 1`. Cross-check the realized LOC of comparable rows in `## Completed` (when present) against the active estimates: a remaining phase of similar scope to a completed one but estimated at a fraction of its realized LOC is a dishonest-estimate signal.
105	
106	7. **Infrastructure-deferral patterns.** This is a distinct failure mode from sunk-cost and must be checked independently. An infrastructure-deferral pattern is present when any of the following hold:
107	
108	   - A route pivot changes the surface approach but the hardest prerequisite (a missing Mathlib construction, an unproven foundational lemma, a typeclass that doesn't exist) is the same before and after the pivot. The pivot renames the problem without solving it. To check: identify the hardest prerequisite in the current route and the hardest prerequisite in the route it replaced. If they are the same construction or the same gap, the pivot is avoidance.
109	
110	   - A required construction is described as "off-critical path", "future work", "deferred pending upstream Mathlib PR", "exceptional case", or similar language, while the project's stated goal provably requires it. The project's goal is not negotiable; if a construction is necessary for the final theorem, it is on the critical path by definition, regardless of what the strategy claims. Check by asking: does the final theorem statement in `## Goal` hold without the deferred construction? If no, the deferral is a goal weakening dressed as a strategic decision.
111	
112	   - The strategy splits the goal into a "main case" and one or more "exceptional cases", proves or plans to prove the main case, and defers the exceptional cases indefinitely. Unless the exceptional cases are genuinely independent theorems not required by `## Goal`, this is a goal weakening. Check the statement in `## Goal` — if it asserts something for ALL cases, each case is required.
113	
114	   - The strategy describes building infrastructure for a weaker or different statement than `## Goal` requires, with the intention of extending later. Plans requiring later extension of core infrastructure almost never complete the extension — flag as CHALLENGE.
115	
116	   - The strategy proposes huge phases and is reluctant to start working on them, without decomposing them into concrete sub-phases that it could have started on. This is a deferral pattern because it will always assume this phase it too difficult, but in pratice it should think like a mathlib contributor and decompose the phase into intermediate phases.
117	
118	   - A phase row in `## Phases & estimations` has `Iters left: ?` (or an estimate-free / never-shrinking cell) AND has been in that state without any active prover lane and without any concrete progress in the blueprints. Stagnant phases are infrastructure-deferral by inaction.
119	
120	   For each infrastructure-deferral finding: name the specific construction being deferred, confirm whether the stated goal requires it, and state whether any route in the strategy actually builds it with a concrete timeline. A construction deferred to "Mathlib upstream" with no project-side plan and no timeline is an unresolved gap in the project's strategy, not an accepted dependency.
121	
122	8. **Parallelism under-exploitation.** If the project has multiple independent files or proof obligations that are not sequentially dependent, but the strategy routes them through a single sequential phase, flag this as a throughput risk. Parallelism in prover dispatch is a planning obligation, not an optimization. A strategy that serializes independent work is implicitly estimating 2–3× the needed iter count.
123	
124	9. **Format compliance.** `STRATEGY.md` must follow the canonical skeleton documented in the plan prompt. Violations to flag:
125	
126	   - **Size**: the file exceeds ~250 lines or ~12 KB.
127	   - **Headings**: the section list isn't exactly `## Goal`, `## Phases & estimations`, `## Completed`, `## Routes`, `## Open strategic questions`, `## Mathlib gaps & new material` (in that order). `## Completed` is optional — omitted while nothing is done yet — but when present it MUST sit between `## Phases & estimations` and `## Routes`. Renamed or other extra top-level sections (`## Project goal`, `## End-state`, `## Decomposition`, `## Roadmap`, `## Soundness rules`, etc.) are violations.
128	   - **Per-iter narrative**: references to specific iterations ("this iter we tried X", "last iter", "the iter-XYZ pivot") in prose. Per-iter history belongs in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, never in STRATEGY.md. (Bare iter numbers in the `## Completed` table's `Iters` cell — e.g. `294 · 8` — are fine; that is the ledger, not narrative.)
129	   - **Accumulation discipline**: a completed phase still sitting in the active `## Phases & estimations` table (it must MOVE to `## Completed`), an excised route still occupying a `## Routes` subsection, or freeform prose-history anywhere. The concise `## Completed` table is allowed and expected; what's flagged is completed work left in the active table, prose bloat, or a `## Completed` table that has ballooned past ~12 rows or grown multi-line cells.
130	   - **Table discipline**: `## Phases & estimations` must be a Markdown table with columns Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks, one short line per cell. `Status` must be a short inline tag (`ACTIVE`, `BLOCKED`, `PAUSED BY USER`, …), not prose; `LOC` must be a rough remaining-LOC range (e.g. `~80–220`), not a velocity figure. When present, `## Completed` must be a Markdown table with columns Phase | Iters (done@ · used) | LOC | Files | Key results | Reusable techniques | Pitfalls, again one short line per cell. Long prose in cells, or replacing either table with prose subsections, is a violation.
131	   - **Appendix sections**: "Historical decisions", "Considered alternatives", "Past iterations summary", "Lessons learned", or any other history-tracking section. Iter sidecars are where rejected alternatives live.
132	
133	   Format violations are reported under a synthetic "format" route — see the Report Format section below. **Format is not cosmetic.** A STRATEGY.md that drifts from the canonical skeleton bleeds into the plan agent's context every iter and makes the strategy itself harder to reason about. Treat material format violations as a CHALLENGE that must be resolved this iter via an in-place restructure (using iter sidecars to hold any historical detail that currently lives inline).
134	
135	## Directive Format
136	
137	```markdown
138	# Strategy Critic Directive
139	
140	## Slug
141	<slug>
142	
143	## Project goal
144	<one paragraph: the final theorem(s) or claim(s) the project is trying to formalize. Include the protected declarations if any.>
145	
146	## Strategy under review
147	
148	<paste the entire STRATEGY.md verbatim.>
149	
150	## References index
151	
152	<paste references/summary.md verbatim.>
153	
154	## Blueprint summary
155	
156	<for each blueprint chapter, one line: filename + topic. NOT the chapter content — just the index.>
157	
158	## Prior critique status
159	
160	<one line per still-live prior-iter challenge. Format STRICTLY:
161	
162	  - <prior-iter-NNN>: <short challenge phrase> — live | addressed
163	
164	NO paraphrased planner responses, NO multi-paragraph re-litigation,
165	NO "the iter-XXX plan-agent considered pulling this to iter-YYY but
166	deferred to iter-ZZZ" — those are iter-by-iter narrative and would
167	contaminate the fresh-context audit. The point of "Prior critique
168	status" is solely to tell you which challenges to re-check, NOT how
169	the planner reasoned about them. If a prior critique is fully resolved
170	in the current STRATEGY.md, mark it "addressed"; if you disagree
171	after auditing, your report will flip it back to live.
172	
173	If first-iter (no prior critique), write "no prior critique".>
174	```
175	
176	**If you find this section contains planner-side reasoning, iter
177	narrative, or paraphrased plan-agent responses beyond the one-line
178	"live | addressed" tags, ignore that extra material.** Your fresh
179	context is your value; do not let leaked planner state colonize
180	your audit.
181	
182	## Report format
183	
184	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/strategy-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
185	
186	**Omit-empty rule.** Every section below is optional except `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict`. If a section's right answer is "nothing to report", **OMIT the section entirely** — do NOT write "none", "N/A", "no findings detected", or "(omit if empty)" as filler content. The absence of a section IS the signal that nothing was found there. Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is SOUND with no flagged items, render the block as just the verdict line and omit the bullet checklist above it.
187	
188	```markdown
189	# Strategy Critic Report
190	
191	## Slug
192	<slug>
193	
194	## Iteration
195	<NNN>
196	
197	## Routes audited
198	
199	For each strategic route in STRATEGY.md, one block:
200	
201	### Route: <name>
202	
203	- **Goal-alignment**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
204	- **Mathematical soundness**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
205	- **Sunk-cost reasoning detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name the sunk-cost claim verbatim>
206	- **Infrastructure-deferral detected**: yes | no — <if yes: name the deferred construction; state whether the goal requires it; state whether any route in the strategy builds it with a concrete timeline>
207	- **Phantom prerequisites**: <list any Mathlib infra the strategy assumes exists that you couldn't verify>
208	- **Effort honesty**: <reasonable | under-counted | over-counted> — <one line>
209	- **Parallelism under-exploited**: yes | no — <if yes, name the independent obligations being serialized>
210	- **Verdict**: SOUND | CHALLENGE | REJECT
211	  - SOUND: the route makes sense and the planner should proceed.
212	  - CHALLENGE: the route has issues the planner must address (in STRATEGY.md or via an explicit rebuttal in plan.md) before this iter ends.
213	  - REJECT: the route is fundamentally broken (goal-misaligned, mathematically unsound, or built on phantom prerequisites). Do not proceed on this route until the strategy is rewritten.
214	
215	## Format compliance
216	
217	A separate block from "Routes audited" — this audits the *document* against the canonical skeleton, regardless of whether the strategic content is sound.
218	
219	- **Size**: <line count> / <bytes> — within budget | over budget (~250 lines / ~12 KB).
220	- **Headings**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, list the violating headings>.
221	- **Per-iter narrative detected**: yes | no — <if yes, quote one or two representative phrases verbatim>.
222	- **Accumulation detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name completed phases still in the active `## Phases & estimations` table (should be in `## Completed`), excised routes still present, prose-history bloat, or a `## Completed` table that has outgrown its bound>.
223	- **Table discipline**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, describe the deviation>.
224	- **Appendix sections**: <list any detected; omit field if none>.
225	- **Format verdict**: COMPLIANT | DRIFTED | NON-COMPLIANT
226	
227	## Infrastructure-deferral findings <!-- omit entire section if no deferral patterns detected -->
228	
229	Each infrastructure-deferral finding gets one block. Default severity CHALLENGE; escalate to REJECT when the deferral makes the stated goal unprovable without the deferred item.
230	
231	### Deferred: <construction name>
232	
233	- **Required by goal**: yes | no | partially — <one line>
234	- **Current plan for building it**: <what STRATEGY.md says, or "none — deferred with no project-side plan">
235	- **Timeline**: <concrete (iter estimate) | vague ("future work") | absent>
236	- **Verdict**: CHALLENGE | REJECT — <one line>
237	
238	## Alternative routes (suggested) <!-- omit entire section if no fresh alternatives -->
239	
240	### Alternative: <name>
241	
242	- **What it looks like**: <one paragraph>
243	- **Why it might be cheaper or sounder**: <one paragraph>
244	- **What the current strategy may have rejected**: <if guessable; otherwise "unclear">
245	- **Severity of the omission**: critical | major | minor
246	
247	## Sunk-cost flags <!-- omit entire section if none -->
248	
249	- `<verbatim quote>` — Why this is sunk-cost: <one sentence>. Recommendation: <reframe on merits>.
250	
251	## Prerequisite verification <!-- omit entire section if no Mathlib infrastructure to verify -->
252	
253	- `<Mathlib name>`: VERIFIED | MISSING | RENAMED (as X)
254	
255	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if zero CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts AND format COMPLIANT AND no infrastructure-deferral findings -->
256	
257	Every CHALLENGE, every REJECT, every NON-COMPLIANT format verdict, and every infrastructure-deferral CHALLENGE/REJECT lands here automatically. No under-classification.
258	
259	- Route <name>: CHALLENGE — <what the planner must address>.
260	- Route <name>: infrastructure-deferral CHALLENGE — <construction> required by goal, no concrete timeline. Planner must either build it this iter or produce a concrete plan with an iter estimate.
261	- Alternative <name>: critical omission — <one line>.
262	- Phantom prerequisite <name>: <one line>.
263	- Format: NON-COMPLIANT — <two or three most impactful deviations>. Restructure STRATEGY.md in-place this iter.
264	
265	## Overall verdict
266	
267	One paragraph. If infrastructure-deferral findings exist, name them explicitly — "the strategy defers X, which is required for the stated goal" must appear verbatim so the plan agent cannot overlook it.
268	```
269	
270	## Return value
271	
272	Your final assistant message:
273	
274	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts, <K> infrastructure-deferral findings, format=<COMPLIANT|DRIFTED|NON-COMPLIANT>`
275	- The path to your full report.
276	
277	## Reminders
278	
279	- **You are the project's adversarial reader.** Don't be polite. If a route is avoidance dressed as strategy, say so.
280	- **Infrastructure deferral is not strategy.** A construction required by the goal is on the critical path regardless of what the strategy labels it.
281	- **A pivot that moves the same hard problem one layer deeper is not a pivot.** Check the hardest prerequisite in the new route against the hardest prerequisite in the old route. If they match, flag it.
282	- **"Off-critical path" is a red flag, not a planning decision**, when the goal requires the deferred item.
283	- **Don't request more context.** Iter history is what you're meant to be free of.
284	- **Cite Mathlib precisely.** Use the LSP tools to verify before claiming a prerequisite exists or is missing.
285	- **Strict severity.** CHALLENGE and REJECT are must-fix; do not under-classify to keep momentum.
286	- **Be specific about alternatives.** "Maybe consider another approach" is useless. Name it.

1	---
2	name: mathlib-analogist
3	description: Read-only Mathlib advisor with two modes. (1) **API alignment** — given a project declaration or proposed design, locates Mathlib's idiom for the same situation, compares the project's path to it, and reports whether the project should align or deviate (with cost analysis). Catches parallel APIs, narrow definitions, and "we couldn't build the prerequisite so we made up a stand-in" patterns before they harden. (2) **Cross-domain inspiration** — when a structural problem (a categorical shape, a fixed-point argument, a sheaf condition, a descent argument, ...) is stuck, searches *distant* Mathlib areas (functional analysis ↔ algebraic geometry ↔ probability ↔ combinatorics) for the same shape and extracts the technique used there.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**,analogies/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - I have two modes. Choose by setting `## Mode: api-alignment` or
10	    `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration` in the directive header.
11	    Dispatch the right mode for the question:
12	
13	    - **api-alignment**: "should this declaration be a typeclass / a
14	      structure / bundled / unbundled?", "does Mathlib already do this
15	      and we're building a parallel?". This is the default and matches
16	      every trigger below under "Proactive" and "Reactive".
17	    - **cross-domain-inspiration**: "this categorical pattern has been
18	      failing for K iters, has Mathlib solved the SAME structural
19	      problem in a different mathematical area?". Use the cross-domain
20	      triggers below.
21	
22	  - In api-alignment mode, I tell you whether Mathlib already does the
23	    same thing the right way and what the cost of NOT aligning would
24	    be. In cross-domain-inspiration mode, I bring back structural
25	    analogues (with citations) and the technique each used, so you
26	    can port it.
27	
28	  ### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)
29	
30	  Dispatch me proactively — this is far cheaper than retroactive
31	  cleanup — when ANY of the following is true:
32	
33	  - You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the
34	    blueprint or have a blueprint-writing subagent (per your catalog)
35	    write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the
36	    Mathlib-aligned version, not a copy.
37	  - You are about to add a new declaration to PROGRESS.md whose type
38	    signature involves a Mathlib namespace you're unfamiliar with.
